First, it's to understand that you can be in one of these three states during a point. You can be on defense, you can be in a neutral rally, or you can be on offense. And we're going to specifically define how you know what state you're in in a second. So in other words, D&O allows you to identify with a specific set of criteria what state you are in. Now second, you can think of D&O as a big toolbox of tactics you can use to transition from defense to neutral and neutral to offense. Now the other key concept here is this is not just some big toolbox and you kind of rummage around and pull out stuff at random. The tactics housed within D&O are specifically designed to transition from defense to neutral or neutral to offense. The progression here for D&O, you always want to go from defense to neutral and neutral to offense. Obviously, you don't want to go in reverse. And also I've got this blue line, no, over to offense. ## [06:01 - 09:02]

It's not going to happen very often. So you first want to neutralize the rally and then work your way up to offense. So there's a very specific progression, again, you're trying to work through. So there's two criteria we're going to use to define whether you're on defense in a neutral rally or an offense. The first one is the quality of shot making. And that's pretty easy to figure out. That's just the quality of your ball, the ball you're hitting relative to the quality of the ball your opponent is hitting. So for example, if you get into a forehand to forehand exchange, we're going to put you down here. Remember, circles the player, stickers the side you're hitting on. This is a forehand variety. And we got your opponent up here. So forehand to forehand exchange. Your forehand is better than your opponent's forehand. You are now on offense. Your opponent is on defense. If your forehands are equal, then it's a neutral rally. And if your forehand isn't as good as your opponent's, if your opponent has a better forehand than you, then you are on defense. Your opponent is on offense. Now keep in mind that this analysis needs to apply to all the shots. It needs to apply to the entire matchup. This is back to swat. So we use swat to define defense, neutral, or offense. You could be in a defensive rally on a forehand to forehand exchange, but you could be on offense backhand to backhand. ## [12:03 - 15:04]

Now what Agassi was really looking to do, and you know, let's take a quick step back. And if we're talking forehand to forehand, then we have this recovery point over there. So Agassi did two things. He went side to side to try and prevent his opponent from getting to the correct recovery points by the time he made contact. So in other words, if Agassi's right here and his opponent is hitting a ball there back cross court, Agassi was trying to make contact before his opponent could get there. And if that happens, well his opponent's a little bit out of position, right? So because his opponent's not positioned properly, then Agassi in this instance is at a slight advantage from a court positioning standpoint. Now Agassi, knowing that, would take whether this was a back hand or a forehand, he would put that ball over here. So his opponent would run over there, hit that ball, send it back cross court to Agassi who is now recovered to the correct point. But maybe this time his opponent's only here by the time Agassi makes contact. So whether Agassi gets a forehand or a backhand, that ball goes over here. So now his opponent has even more distance to cover and you can see over the series of several shots, his opponent gets further and further out of position to the point where his opponent could basically be in the boonies by the time Agassi is making contact. And Agassi just has to put the ball in the open court, doesn't have to do too much, you know, in terms of the quality of the shot, can just put the ball over there. So most of the balls are going to be somewhere in here in the middle of the court. Other important point from a court positioning standpoint, while a pusher is typically a good mover, they stand very far behind the baseline so that they have time to run down a lot of balls.
